institution: Sector-wide typology - Indian retail digital payments
source_documents: https://www.npci.org.in (UPI circulars - collect-request limits, fraud advisories)
category: Digital payment fraud - UPI collect-request social engineering
risk_signals: Victim receives an unexpected UPI 'collect' (pull-payment) request framed as a refund, prize, or incoming payment
Instruction to 'enter your UPI PIN to receive money' - receiving never requires a PIN
Request originates from a marketplace/classifieds deal (OLX-style) or an urgent refund pretext
analyst_reasoning: A UPI collect request is a request to PAY, not receive. Fraudsters exploit the confusion by disguising a debit request as an incoming credit, so approving it with a PIN debits the victim. The tell is that any flow asking for a PIN to 'receive' money is inverted from how UPI works.
investigation_steps: Pull the victim's UPI transaction log and identify approved collect requests and their payee VPAs
Check the reputation/age of the payee Virtual Payment Address and whether it is newly created
Correlate the timing with a marketplace contact or refund pretext
decision: Block the payee VPA, alert the customer that PIN entry is never needed to receive money, and file a complaint / cyber-crime report promptly to attempt recall of funds.
outcome: Funds are debited and often quickly layered onward; recovery depends on speed of reporting and the receiving bank freezing the mule account.
lessons_learned: Receiving money on UPI never requires entering a PIN - PIN entry always authorises a debit
Collect requests from unknown VPAs tied to marketplace deals are a red flag
Speed of reporting is the single biggest driver of fund recovery
